stem: Which process is responsible for T2 relaxation?

options.text: Recovery of longitudinal magnetization through energy transfer to the lattice
Spatial misregistration of fat and water during readout
Replacement of transverse magnetization by a stronger gradient field
Loss of transverse phase coherence from spin-spin interactions

explanation: T2 relaxation is transverse decay caused by microscopic spin-spin interactions that progressively dephase the spins. T1 describes longitudinal recovery and energy exchange with the surrounding lattice.

stem: At one tissue T2 time after excitation, approximately how much transverse magnetization remains?
explanation: T2 is the time required for transverse magnetization to decay to about 37% of its initial value. The complementary 63% figure is commonly associated with longitudinal recovery after one T1.

stem: Why does the free induction decay usually disappear faster than the tissue's true T2 decay?

options.text: It measures only longitudinal recovery after the RF pulse
It includes both irreversible spin-spin dephasing and reversible field inhomogeneity effects
It is acquired after all transverse magnetization has been refocused
It excludes susceptibility and main-field variations

explanation: The observed FID follows T2*, which combines intrinsic T2 decay with additional dephasing from B0 inhomogeneity and susceptibility. A spin echo can refocus much of the reversible component.

stem: Which tissue property generally produces high signal on a strongly T2-weighted image?

options.text: A short T2 with rapid transverse dephasing
A short T1 with no transverse magnetization
A long T2 with substantial transverse magnetization remaining at the echo
A low proton density combined with a long echo time

explanation: Tissues with long T2 values retain transverse coherence later into the sequence and therefore generate more signal at a long TE. Free water is the classic example of a bright T2-weighted material.

stem: What dephasing can a 180-degree refocusing pulse substantially reverse?

options.text: Longitudinal recovery caused by T1 relaxation
Diffusion-related motion during every applied gradient
Dephasing caused by static magnetic field differences

explanation: The 180-degree pulse reverses the relative positions of faster and slower spins, allowing static frequency offsets to rephase at the echo. It cannot recover signal already lost through irreversible T2 interactions.
